integrity is at the heart of what we do.

across the public sector.
Um, we do have the SFO,
but we've seen them take a near $1 million cut in their
budget, uh, from 2025 to 2026.
Um, they say on repeat that their job isn't really to be
that blanket coverage or to monitor.
Um, we've got the OAG reports,
which occasionally flag issues, um, in more
of a naming and shaming way.
And then we have some cases that are taken to the courts.
But my observation is there's quite a high bar threshold,
um, of what's deemed to be an appropriate there,
which is quite a contrast with the Australian system
wherein, um, 2018 they put in a judicial review
of government procurement act in place.
So two parts to the question.
Um, firstly, what is the, um, what is the process
or monitoring of procurement from government's perspective,
and then the monitoring of that?
Um, and is there a need for something that's, um,
less soft in terms of guidelines and policies
and more firm in terms of legislative change
that could aid in, um, ensuring
that there's sunlight over procurement?
Um, look, I, I think that one of those, some
of those questions are best for the, uh, functional lead
for procurement that sits in mb 'cause that's the expertise.
What I can talk about though, is some of the
work we've been doing to, um, off the back of
what we've seen around procurement
and the public service itself.
But I dunno if that gets to your, I think
that wouldn't get to your question.
I think we would recognize that,
and this is a matter of public record, that procurement,
that there have been issues on procurement, uh,
particularly in local government.
But that, so we remain
like there's a very high level of awareness.
Secondly, we do rely as the commission
on MV leading that,
but each agency has an audit every year.
We, we check the internal controls, we check the
separation of duties, very traditional ways of making sure
that no one person has the ability to hide,
you know, ill-gotten gains for want of a better word.
So all of that housekeeping is in place,
but we collectively feel
that it's gonna continue to come under increased pressure
because the stakes are getting higher.
You know, that there's some social economic issues here
for some of these people for making the decision.
So we, we are strengthening the processes,
we are strengthening the leadership
and we're strengthening the audit.
Mm-hmm. But I couldn't give you a guarantee
that there isn't a mischief at any point in time.
And we have to minimize that to the best of our ability.
I I I,
I was just gonna ask if there's a view on whether the
external, um, mechanisms for accountability are sufficient.
We, we think so, but we,
this whole area is, as I said earlier, very dynamic.
So we, we are not sitting passively thinking we are okay,
because we've now got a code of conduct,
or we've got the auditors.
It, it is, it, it is much more dynamic than that.
So we remain as a system alert to
where the gaps are in strengthening in its best we can.
And I think, as Hugo said earlier, we are also, uh,
I think looking at a much more risk-based approach.
Not, not all risks are equal, if that makes sense. Mm-hmm.
Procurement is an Achilles heel because there has cases
and there will continue to be cases,
and a lot of money goes through the public sector
and it goes to third parties.
And we have to have confidence that that's done according
to the ethics that we would expect.
And, and sorry. And if we find anything that it,
that it is prosecutors
and that, you know, these are matters for police,
but if there's fraud that we have zero tolerance for that.
There isn't a materiality if you breached it, you have to
be exited and you'll be prosecuted.
And I just wanted to add a kind
of general theme of the briefing.
I, I think, which is relevant here is that yes,
it's very important to have, you know, oversight
and control in those functions like the serious fraud office
and kind of yearly audits
and accountability mechanisms to make sure that you are kind
of, I identifying a problem where it arises.
But no matter how much you put into those functions,
you're never going to, um, be able to, um,
get the, uh, it's equally important, um,
to put in place the right, uh, put in place levers
that will help you to build the right sort
of behaviors and cultures.
So proactively try
and make sure that you are getting, you know, people
to understand, um, the right things to do.
And so we've got our general approach to, to building that
through codes of conduct and integrity.
But likewise, the MB procurement lead works really hard
to put in place, you know, good standards
and guidance for agencies about the processes
that they put in place and what's expected of their people
to make sure that they're
building the right sorts of culture.
And just a very quick follow up and,
and just as we had done law in front of us who talked about,
um, the use of deep fakes and evidence
and this new issue that they're hitting there now,
in the same way we can talk about fraud emerging across the
public sector and what we need to monitor for,
is there an increasing in expertise in terms of monitoring
for these new types of technology?
Yes, there is, but it's not universal across the system.
And, and that exposes us
And, and I just, I think the serious fraud office has
dedicated their entire long term briefing
and, uh, their long term insights briefing on the topic
of using tech, uh, increasing use, use of technology
to detect fraud and corruption.
So there's think further deep thinking
going into that issue.
Thank you. Mostly, oh, that's, I just wanna make,
make a comment before I shout.
It's really exciting that people are using technology
to actually predict
where the crime's actually coming and actually detect.
I think that's one problem we're using it.
Um, we were talking about integrity,
and one of the things that I think Parliament does is
reflecting the public in terms
of the population base is also part of the integrity.
So people actually feel like they belong in New Zealand.
Mm-hmm. How do you think public service actually fares in
terms of reflecting the population in the people
that actually, uh, become public servants?
I, I think we may have addressed the previous meeting.
I, I, we, we sort of feel, um,
are we absolutely reflective in a percentage sense?
Probably not, but actually we are well represented across
all ethnic groups.
Mm-hmm. Um, and that diversity is a strength for the system.
Um, and so can we do better?
I, I would think generally yes, we can,
but you know, as we recruit, we do look for diversity,
but it's also gotta be merit based. Mm-hmm. So,
Of course, I I wouldn't want it any
Other, we're managing that.
I think we are managing that well.
Um, but it's,
I I would definitely describe it as work in progress.
I think when it comes to that diversity of our staff,
that's one of the factors
that we think about when we think about
induction and training.
As we get different cultural groupings into
the public sector, we need to equip them to understand
what it means when we talk about codes of conduct,
when we talk about ethics,
when we talk about independence, all of those things.
And it's not to be disparaging, it's just everybody has
to have the same level of understanding of what is required.
And I don't think we've always done that.
Hmm. Do you, I'm not looking at numbers
or percentages of representation.
What I'm actually looking at is what is the barrier?
Have you actually had a discussion
or, you know, have that discussion actually happened
with people who are applying or fail
or, you know, trying to figure out
whether there is a, in fact a barrier?
You know, we often talk about the glass
ceiling for different things.
Yeah. I mean, is there a glass ceiling for public service
that prevent, um, people
who actually potentially come from other countries
who actually might want to be, uh, in New Zealand,
but actually have the ability to serve,
but they don't actually apply?
Uh, so there has been work that's been done
to look at the, how recruitment is framed in order to, uh,
try and address some of those barriers.
Mm. To ensure that we are looking
for the breadth of experience.
Um, so, you know, being quite careful about sort
of recruitment to ensure that we try
and recruit from the widest, uh, portion.
In terms of, um, you know,
there has been a shift in the public service workforce
makeup, the demographic makeup.
It has shifted quite a lot
where the big areas I think has been discussed before,
but we can provide data has been
around senior leadership now.
There has been, uh, change there too.
Uh, but the more changes needed, um, uh,
but certainly in the public service as a whole,
it has actually shifted over the last, uh, 10 years
and actually the last six years in particular,
where we've been much more aware of some
of the barriers created by the way that we drive,
do our job descriptions, some of the, um,
requirements that we have.
So we try and make sure we also get o other experience, um,
but then have to go through a merit based pro process so
that we get the best person for the position, which is
what the legislation, legislation says we must do.
I think, like Brian said,
even though you're not interested in, in percentages,
and I think it is indicative, you know, the fact
that we are broadly a representative
of the population from a statistical perspective
does indicate that people are able
to enter the public service from all
different walk walks that we have.
But, you know, that we would not ever wish
to see there be barriers to get the best people
with the greatest diversity
Into the public sector.
In terms of barriers, I, is there, um, a perceived, um,
barrier to, uh, choose certain people
concerning the fact that there's actually, you know,
global concern in terms of foreign interference, uh, the,
you know, bad actors are out there.
So what kind of, uh,
work do you actually do when you're actually screening?
Or sorry, in, in the, in the, uh, training of people
who actually are, um, actually already in the public service
to be aware, for example? Yeah,
So we do two things.
So there's the workforce assurance standards, which have,
uh, significantly strengthened the requirements
for agencies, particularly about referee checking, uh,
and more robust referee checking.
And now includes questions around integrity, also
around misconduct, uh, internally.
So, so there, there's been a lot
of work in the public service over the last few years to,
um, to strengthen the, the sort
of upfront screening that happens.
Uh, internally we've been talking
with the integrity champions around, uh, some of the flags
that you are wanting to be looking out for.
So some of those flags might be people not wanting to take,
uh, leave in certain periods because they're not wanting to,
because they've got something sort
of nefarious perhaps going on.
Uh, there are a range of agencies
that have quite good monitoring around patterns of behavior.
Uh, so, um, we are quite alert to, uh, sort
of changes and we're trying to alert managers
to think about changes in behavior.
We have a continuous requirement to,
of disclosure when things change in people's lives.
Mm-hmm. So there is a lot of work going on.
There's some work going on that I can't really talk about,
but, um, we, yeah,
We remain extremely alert.
That's very good. And,
and of course there's always, you know,
dependent on the role, there's, there's always the,
the option to have, uh, formal
security clearance requirements,
and that's a much thorough,
much more thorough vetting process as well.
So I think this is senior roles particularly. Yeah.
And that goes to the heart of why we did this briefing,
is we're part of a global community.
We are not immune from any of those behavioral trends,
and we have to protect ourselves before it's too late.
So, quick set from Andy
and then we'll just for my,
so just making sure we have enough time
For you. Just thinking of that global
community, I mean,
we've had quite a number of fairly high profile, um,
situations where people have been recruited, um,
from overseas.
Uh, and they've had, you know,
they've obviously been reliance on, um, uh,
on references from overseas.
Um, and, uh, some
of 'em come spectacularly at drift at significant cost
to other people, including high level people within public
service itself who've had to bear that cost in terms of that
checking process, make sure that it is robust.
Have there been lessons learned from that to make sure
that it is absolutely minimized in future?
Yes. We, we, we like to think
of ourselves as a learning system,
and when we've had bad experiences, we've adapted modified.
I, I think, and, you know, the most recent,
although not international, but you know, the Jeff
and mc skimming experience has really sharpened the focus
and the breadth of what we do now testing.
Um, we do run the risk
of people have alternative lives, it's quite hard to break
that into it.
Um, so we've gotta get a balance between
respecting the rights of an individual,
but respecting the needs of the system.
You know, I think it's an ongoing challenge
and one that will, over time,
I think there will be more intrusion into the role people
want to be a chief executive.
They're going to be exposed
to more scrutiny than would otherwise be the case.
We've also, uh, engaged internationally
with our counterparts
to test whether there are additional things we can do, um,
and to see how we compare, uh, all the changes we've done,
uh, actually quite
actually more robust than our international
counterparts is what we found.
Uh, there are some things that we can adopt from our
international counterparts, um,
but, uh, we've really wanted to test whether we were
an outlier, but actually it turns out that we're actually,
uh, putting in place now a whole lot of additional checks,
particularly for senior leaders, uh,
that put us quite in front.
You know, we, we are strongly the view
that if we get the best quality people,
we'll get a good quality outcome.
The process isn't gonna protect us. Yeah.
Very quick, very quick stuff. I do need to get
Just a supplementary on that. So
Maybe a quick answer to it.
I know you actually said there is a differential between,
you know, what we do as a job
and in the private life there must be certain point
where the personal life can actually create a problem.
So when do you actually, uh, at
what point do you actually investigate?
Um, I'm thinking like, for example,
I've actually seen instances where people have belonged
to a cult, for example, this is not a New Zealand example.
And, um, and that impacts on the work that they actually do.
Um, you know, and just wanna know if where,
where you would actually interfere or investigate.
Do you have a trigger point?
Well, I mean, it, it's actually a matter
for normal employment law about generally whether you're
bringing your employer into disrepute is often the kind
of angle, it's not a political neutrality or other angle.
It could be a co obviously a code of conduct question.
But there are normal employment law for an private
or public employer where your personal activities actually
undermine the reputation of an employer
or your ability to do your job.
But it's not my area of expertise, it's probably others.
It just trigger when the memory of
It's if, if, if
what you do in your non time impacts on your ability
to discharge your job
and all the obligations of that job,
then that comes to our attention.
We would investigate
and we would take whatever action is required.
Yeah.
Okay. Like David in
Goes, um, this, the commission's mandate doesn't extend
through to public servants within the local
government sector.
Um, I guess what, what risk do you see I guess, in taking
that siloed approach to different tier of government?
Uh, I don't know whether it's a siloed approach.
I mean, the commission's mandate has always been, uh,
related to the core public service, uh, values.
Uh, local government is slightly different.
It's a devolved sort of sector of government.
Um, and, you know,
it doesn't necessarily have quite the same context.
Um, so I mean, it has been discussed in the past,
but it would be a significant, uh, shift in,
uh, I mean, where you want the commission involved is
where we have specialized knowledge and ability.
And that is about
what makes the public service quite unique,
which is political neutrality, free
and frank advice, you know, those sorts of things.
So local government is much, much broader.
There is the OAG that has, uh,
has an oversight role in relation
to the local government sector.
The local government itself has legislation,
also has relevant codes.
It's also a devolved form of sort of democratic governance.
So there are some difficult questions in there about whether
you would expand what is an executive function of government
to a local delegated local government authority.
And I think you'd probably have a separate kind
of system for that person. Yeah.
What what we do know though is our guidance is used
by a lot of local government entities
and broader government entities.
Or even though we may not have a direct ability to control
and direct, they are using our material
to inform their own independence.
And how that,
In some ways you could consider that,
that it's inherent in, in the, the highest level
of governance, that there is a siloization there.
That the local government is not
accountable to central government.
They're separately democratically elected.
And so they are separate systems to an extent,
But there shouldn't be any daylight between what we adopt
and what they Sure. Thanks.
Thank you. I just had some, um, questions around the, uh,
public service census results,
which you have included in, in the report.
And I know that this is something that I think was one
of the first things the minister picked up
after the census was around the, uh, statistics in relation
to bullying and harassment with, with only 14%
of public servants Yeah.
Satisfied with how, uh, their, a complaint was resolved
and only 52% reported bullying harassment,
even though they experienced it.
Those are concerning figures,
and I think that's concern has been shown, shown across, um,
the political spectrum.
Um, and it is an integrity issue
because it goes to integrity of our workplaces.
So, um, what, uh, specific
actions are being put in place since you received those
results in order to address those concerning results?
I can talk To. Yeah.
I, I mean, maybe just firstly,
and I think we said this, you know, the concept
of not having a safe workplace is, is abhorrent, really.
And the idea that we have bullying
and harassment in our workplaces, uh,
was both shocking and disappointing.
And so as a result, we have got a number
of initiatives underway.
Yeah. Um, so one of the, uh,
core changes we've made has been to the,
um, speaking up guidance.
So we already had speaking up, which is one
of the most important ways that public servants are able to
speak up about wrongdoing as, as well as
through the protected disclosure regime.
But we made it very clear that people could come to us.
Uh, 'cause often if you are, uh, not,
if you are experiencing something in your organization,
you may have low trust in that organization.
Previously it, it just went up the line
to the chief executive.
So we've made that, uh, change
and that's had us quite a, uh, increase in volume
of complaints coming to us.
We did already receive complaints.
Uh, the second piece we've done has been, uh,
a supplementary, uh, which is your complaints,
your right rights, uh, guidance.
Uh, so that sets out what complainants
and what respondents can expect, uh,
that includes in some instances
where there's significant imbalance of power.
Yeah. The agencies consider providing, uh, legal support.
Uh, so particularly we've had, um,
quite some in the past some issues with complainants, uh,
who've experienced sexual harassment.
Uh, who, um, uh, the through
that process actually got damaged through
that process itself.
And if they'd had some proper legal support, uh, that,
that we would've avoided, that we've checked our guidance
with people who've experienced that
and had, uh, support for that.
So we've added that into it.
I also think the protected disclosure regime,
those changes have been really important
to provide protections.
Uh, we actually treat every speaking up as if it, you know,
we essentially try and, uh, ensure anonymity
and protect people, uh, through that process.
So we've, we are also doing training through the, um,
integrity champion network.
And then there's the, um, health
and safety workplace leads are also treating it as a,
as an example of psychosocial.
And every chief executive, um, has been required
to get their own response plan to the bit
of the census that related to them.
And this is one of the things, once again
with the chief executives, we talk about guidance,
but you know, this is a non-negotiable point.
You have to make your workplace safe
and people need to feel that there is an, an,
an appropriate balance of power.
Just, um, finally, almost out of time, um,
there are significant number of, uh, recommendations, uh,
or options, uh, in the latter part of the report mm-hmm.
Over the next 12 months.
Uh, which of those recommendations will you be following
Through with?
Um, so there are a range of, um,
'cause the briefing can't provide
recommendation, you know, options.
Yeah. Options, options, options.
So we are picking up on a number of those including, uh,
some of the suggestions from the auditor general
as well, OAG.
Um, so, uh, we have a, um,
currently have an action plan, which we publicly report on
that includes, uh, obviously the, you know, the, uh,
taking forward the reset of behaviors, which is one
of the actions and the training, uh, we're adding in.
Um, we're working at the moment.
Uh, we've got, I think it's, we've, um, provided a response.
We've got a, uh,
and we've got a, developed a sort of a strategy
that we're under underway internally,
but we're working on that risk-based model.
So we can more, more closely target, uh, areas
of risk in terms of i procurement such as procurement.
Uh, we've also, um, taken on board, uh, we,
we've developed a new measurement framework,
which we'll publish on.
Uh, we're just working on that.
We've got, uh, we've got about 50%
of the data we already have.
We have additional data that we've sourced, uh,
or are sourcing from across the system,
which is already collected.
And then there's a bit of new data that we are going to have
to collect, uh, which we've got underway.
So that's the sort of measurement and reporting side of it.
Uh, so that's going to give us a lot more. Um,
But if it's helpful to the committee,
we can give you a more detailed briefing about
what our action plan is
and what the sequence of events is likely to be.
I mean, it, it is, as I said earlier, it's,
it's not something, you know, we are very active.
It's not a passive exercise,
and it, it just reflects the sense
of urgency based on global experience that we just have
to lift our game and be more diligent.
Thank you. Well, and now out of time.
So thank you, thank you three very much for joining us
and for all actively participating in the hearing
and also for this, um, excellent report.
And thank you. Thank you very much.
And it is any follow up questions? Yeah. Appreciate it.
Thank you.

Wednesday 3 March 2026 | Governance and Administration Committee

Public Service Commission, Long-Term Insights Briefing 2025

Public Service Commission
- Sir Brian Roche, Public Service Commissioner
-- Hugo Vitalis, Deputy Chief Executive – Strategy, Policy & Integrity
- Callum Butler, Manager, Policy – General Public Administration
